channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: WOLFHOUND Preview - Wolfenvania
item.content:encoded:
Sci-fi Metroidvanias are a dime a dozen, but I can't think of any with a World War 2 setting. That is, until WOLFHOUND. I'm grateful for that, because the genre feels a bit saturated at the moment. Something fresh is needed, and WOLFHOUND might just be the answer.

In WOLFHOUND, you infiltrate a mysterious island in the Bermuda Triangle. The Nazis are embedded in the island, with a large fortress towering over a lush jungle. Seems like a job for Captain Chuck Rossetti, an all-American badass. Turns out, Nazis are just the tip of the iceberg. This island is full of much more than Allied intelligence would suggest.

Captain Rossetti, AKA WOLFHOUND

WOLFHOUND is as authentic a Metroidvania as it gets. It's very old-school, from the 8-bit, retro art style down to the gameplay. Because of this, it's instantly familiar and easy to jump into without learning a bunch of convoluted mechanics; still, despite its simplicity, it's no less fun than that of its counterparts. 

This demo build I previewed comprises two major environments and a few boss battles. Along the way, I collected various upgrades to help me traverse new obstacles and access areas I couldn't reach earlier -- pretty standard fare. What I like is how grounded WOLFHOUND is so far. 

Despite the giant bugs, zombies, and Nazis our character encounters, all of the upgrades thus fit the World War 2 theme. Tactical pads -- you know, knee pads -- are used to roll through smaller gaps. Gotta protect those knees, after all.

Likewise, the climbing gloves allow the Captain to grasp onto ledges and cracks in the wall. Now you're able to scale cliffs and sort of wall jump, given you have the proper handholds. You still feel like a normal dude, not some super soldier in a special suit like Samus Aran or the like. 

And WOLFHOUND promises to provide a number of different WW2-era weapons, as well. You start out with a basic pistol, which has unlimited ammo. Think of this as Samus' blaster, except it requires reloading. Then there's the bolt-action rifle, which has a longer range single projectile with more power to it. I later found a shotgun, but due to its closer range, I didn't find it quite as useful.

Either way, whatever weapon you choose, these can be enhanced further with weapon kits. You can upgrade a weapon's attributes, such as damage, which is great to see -- it means that even the earlier weapons don't fall off later on, so you're able to keep using your preferred guns that cater to your play style. 

The caveat is that you can only hold your sidearm and one larger gun from the start. There's also a second slot for weapons, so presumably, you'll be able to utilize your pistol, bolt-action rifle, and shotgun (or whatever other weapons you find) and switch between the three of them. As I understand it, we'll also gain access to a flamethrower, Thompson, and even a railgun later on. 

A Mysterious Island

Map design was about as well-made as one would come to expect for a Metroidvania. It strikes a nice balance between platforming and enemy placement, keeping me on my toes constantly. Mines are scattered all around the island, and it's easy to miss them when you're focused on that giant bug or Nazi ahead of you.

In one early instance, there's a long, linear path of graves that constantly spawned zombies. At first, this proved troublesome, with the zombies leaping forward and surrounding me quickly. Luring them into the mines was a nice way to avoid some danger, since you'll have to otherwise mag dump on these undead foes. 

All too often, at least for modern Metroidvanias, I find that maps are just one long, linear pathway. Sure, there's some verticality to these maps and hidden areas, but nothing too crazy. WOLFHOUND's developer, Bit Kid, did its homework. This feels like a legit, authentic map that goes in all directions, which is great for those who love exploring and not simply beelining to the next area.

Indeed, I came across a Nazi castle after riding a lift high up cliff, which felt like the natural continuation of the first jungle area. It was a dead end, with a gate barring the way. To access this area, you'll actually have to go below, into these crystal mines full of weird, mutated miners and drill machines.

By navigating this tricky crystal mine -- which, as it turns out, is inside the cliff and reaches far above the jungle but also below it as well -- you're able to get into the underbelly of this castle. Alas, that's where my demo ended, but it was at that point that WOLFHOUND really hooked me. 

WOLFHOUND Preview | Final Thoughts

I'm impressed with WOLFHOUND. From my perception, it doesn't aim to reinvent the wheel. Rather, WOLFHOUND aims to be a competent, well-made Metroidvania that provides a great, nostalgic experience full of challenge and exploration. And so far? It's done pretty well. I'm very excited to see what else it has to offer. 

WOLFHOUND was previewed on PC with a code provided by the publisher over 2 hours of gameplay - all images were taken during the preview process.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/wolfhound-preview-wolfenvania
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Sam Reich Explains Game Changer: Home Edition's Modular Approach, How To Play, and Why Their Kickstarter Is Also a Game
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday Dropout.tv launched a Kickstarter for Game Changer: Home Edition, a board game version of their popular game show where the gimmick is that the game the contestants are playing changes every episode. With the Game Changer: Home Edition, fans will be able to play Bingo, Name A Number, and Sam Says from the comfort of their own homes.

We got to sit down and talk with Sam Reich, CEO of Dropout and host of Game Changer, just hours after the launch of the Kickstarter to learn more about each of the modular games, how the approach to prompts was different when creating a board game, and how they came up with the community challenges that replace the traditional stretch-goal content unlock format. 

To top off the interview, I had to congratulate Reich on the Kickstarter, which had an initial goal of $40,000, for reaching $1 million in funding in about exactly two hours after launch.

After a long pause of bewilderment, Reich broke the silence, "Thanks, Andy, what else are you supposed to say? It's sincerely hard to comprehend that amount of money, let alone 7,500 people." Reich paused to recheck the Kickstarter page. "Excuse me, 7,745."

Asking how closely he was watching the numbers rise, he simply said: "How could you not take advantage of the dopamine?"

To start at the top of the idea itself, I asked Reich how the Home Edition came to be and how long this had been in the works. "You know, we really aren't die-hard strategists. The reason why now is because we opportunized on figuring this out finally."

Talking on the history of converting Game Changer to a product fans could play at home, Reich said, "For years, we've obviously had the opportunity to do this. The Game Changer fandom has been building a lot over the last few years, but it's a complicated creative challenge to try to turn Game Changer into a home game or party game.

"We had been taking pitches off and on for a while, and I didn't feel like anyone had really nailed it, and then Joshua Balvin comes along and pitches us a version of the game that's very in sync with my ambitions for the game as a business, but also incredibly creatively cohesive. We played through his mock of it as a writers' room and were like, 'Oh my god, there's a real game here.' And then we all started iterating together on prompts, and it was ready now."

Reich chuckled, almost in disbelief. "I mean, that is to say, we really did want the game to be fully realized by the time we launched it on Kickstarter. We didn't want to launch with something half-baked and then figure it out, particularly because we thought, 'If the fans all rush to support this thing, we want to make sure we're going to be able to deliver on it.' Right here, now staring at this $1.2 million number, I'm so happy we did that."

"Knowing that I have something I'm really proud of to be able to share with all these people is super exciting." - Sam Reich

Game Changer: Home Edition Is a Modular Game With Room to Grow

Diving into the game itself, I was interested to learn how each of the modules worked. "It's a modular game, which I was really excited to build because I want this to be something we can continue to invest in.

"This is NOT a one-and-done kinda thing," Reich warned. "In my ideal world, this is a modular game where we can eventually come out with future modules, and folks can basically swap out modules to create their own changing Game Changer experience."

Talking about playing the current version of Game Changer: Home Edition, Reich explained, "A full game takes place over three rounds, with a game per round. This initial launch packet consists of three games, the first of which is Bingo, the second is Name A Number, and the third is Sam Says. All three of those have been reimagined from the show into games that will make sense in party-game atmosphere.

"Bingo is a guess-the-word game where you are trying to get your team to guess the word on your card without saying any of the words that the opposing team has written down that you might also say." Comparing it to a game that a fan of party games might have familiarity with, Reich elaborated, "Imagine you playing a version of Celebrity where you could set off a landmine."

Deeper gameplay strategy immediately comes into play when you're not just playing the game, but the person across from you. "If the opposing team knows you very well, it's all the more likely I'm going to set off those landmines."

"Name A Number is basically a stunt game where you are signing up to do something a certain number of times without knowing what that something is. Once you look at it you can pass it to the opposing team, but once you do, they're going to be able to do it for less or more times depending on what's more challenging."

Some examples of challenges that can be seen on Kickstarter are to "Flip the sand timer. You must flip a coin and get # heads before time runs out" or "Get outside and back inside within # seconds" where # is the number that you or another player proposes.

"Sam Says is a very difficult follow-the-rules type game. With each one of these games, my writers' room has been actively involved in writing prompts, which has been really, really fun to do."

Prompts Crafted to Support Humor

With the prompts being created in his writers' room, I asked, when compared to the standard prompts that are written for Game Changer or similar improv games on Dropout like Make Some Noise, which are crafted for professional improv comedians, how were the prompts for the Home Edition approached?

"It is different," Reich began, "I think the Dropout/Game Changer fandom is funny, but we also want for this game to reach people who don't consider themselves all that funny."

"The burden of comedy has to be more so on us than on the player."

"We also want the players to be able to show up with their creativity, so that's a tough balance. In Game Changer, we primarily are designing games that are fun to watch; it actually doesn't matter if they're that fun to play. They might even be torturous to play… here they have to be primarily fun to play," Reich said.

"There's also the challenge that we did not want to come up with a 'hosted' game. We wanted to come up with a game that everyone could play together. With the host role taken out, that requires some tweaking too. It is in that sense a bit of a translation that takes place into the home game but I really think that we've nailed it. I think it will be evident in the Parlor Room and Smosh Games episodes that are coming out in the next few weeks. It's really fun."

Building to a Big Finale

Knowing that one of my favorite aspects of Game Changer is in the escalation, starting with a bit and making it bigger or even inverting it, I asking if the prompts were set up at all in a way that would cater to that.

With a big smile across his face, Reich explained: "The answer to your question is just 'no.'

"There are more and less challenging prompts for sure. You know, part of the challenge in designing the home game is that you do know the rules when you get started. How we're doing that in a way that remains fun and surprising for the audience, and the answer is that you may know what the rules are, but you don't know what the prompts are that we're asking you to do."

"Particularly in [Sam Says and Name A Number], the prompts are going to ask you to do some pretty outrageous things. Now, if you play Bingo, followed by Name A Number, followed by Sam Says, you are playing in an escalating order of zaniness. So that's the order that we suggest."

"Bingo is the most straightforward, Name A Number is medium to high zany, and then Sam Says is like Name A Number on steroids."

Community Challenges to Unlock New Content

It wouldn't be a Game Changer Kickstarter if the Kickstarter itself weren't a game. Instead of traditional funding goals to unlock additional content, there are 60+ challenges that will be put to the community. Some that we've already seen are for 250 people to comment with a haiku, posting 200+ photos posing as Y, M, C, or A, and recreating the Game Changer logo with edible materials.

"We're working with some talented folks to be able to pull this Game Changer campaign off. Our feeling was that we couldn't in good conscience launch our Game Changer campaign without the campaign itself feeling Game Changer-y in nature, which is ambitious because, on the one hand, we're already launching a game that we had to come up with, and now we're launching challenges related merely to the campaign.

"It's a lot of work. Listen Andy, we just did the work, spoiler alert, there's over 60 of these Kickstarter challenges, which like, if day one is any indication, we're probably going to get through them."

Reich teased that "some of them are meaningfully more challenging," letting the question slip, "Are we going to get a backer in Antarctica? I don't know… but it was really fun coming up with those."

Talking a bit more seriously, Reich said that "fundraising is a little bit of a capitalist exercise, and this helps take some of the harder edges off of that. Where it's like 'Our Stretch Goals are fun,' it's not merely to do with fundraising."

I could definitely understand the want to not just have the campaign be about a big number getting even bigger, and I am sure there are so many Game Changer fans ravenous to take part in a game that Reich is hosting.

Speaking of the secondary benefit of the Community Challenges, Reich also pointed out, "the fact that these will happen will help amplify the campaign. But some of them are like so silly," Reich broke out into laughter.

"We're working with a team called Popular Demand on fulfilling the Kickstarter campaign portion itself and the fine folks at Popular Demand are helping us. They both championed this and also are helping us to actually make sure we run it fairly, as difficult as that will be."

I did warn Reich that he'll have to start preparing for interest in flights to Antarctica to rise as that Community Goal is announced…

Another Community Goal that Reich revealed was "get my dad to donate to the campaign."

"I'm looking forward to the day when tens of thousands of Dropout fans are sicced on him," Reich said.

Sam Reich's father, Robert Reich, who has appeared on Dropout/Game Changer a few times, is a professor, author, lawyer, and political commentator who worked in the administrations of Presidents Gerald Ford and Jimmy Carter, was the Secretary of Labor for President Bill Clinton, and was also a member of the economic transition advisory board for President Barack Obama.

As Reich pulled a glass up to his face to take a sip of water, he added: "I didn't ask for his approval or anything."

As a final nugget of information, as I was thanking Reich for his time and expressing my anticipation to get my hands on the game, Reich told me, "I hope you like it. I'm a big fan of it, and believe it or not… we're already planning Box Two."



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/features/sam-reich-explains-game-changer-home-editions-modular-approach-how-to-play-and
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Eve Online Developer CCP Games Rebrands to Fenris Creations
item.content:encoded:
Eve Online developer CCP Games has rebranded itself to Fenris Creations, and the company has also announced that it's partnering up with Google's DeepMind AI division to "explore new gameplay experiences" powered by the division's technologies.

Per a press release, Fenris Creations, which recently split from former parent company Pearl Abyss after a management buyout, will return to "a model similar to how the company operated before 2018", namely one "designed to support strategic decision-making for persistent live games and long-running virtual worlds".

If you're worried about this change affecting the day-to-day operations of Eve Online, you needn't be, it seems; Fenris says its "leadership team, studios, products, and ongoing development plans" are unchanged, and the same people who have shepherded Eve for many years are still at the helm.

As part of the rebrand, Fenris also shows off some of the projects on which it's currently working, including Eve Online (naturally), as well as space survival sim Eve Frontier and the studio's proprietary Carbon engine.

Another project shown on the banner is Eve Vanguard, described by Fenris as "an extraction-adventure FPS" in which you'll do the usual extraction shooter things, namely exploring, looting, and getting the heck out of Dodge.

Conspicuous by its absence, however, is the shooter announced in conjunction with developer Sperasoft back in 2020. It seems like that project is still missing in action, although it is, of course, possible that we'll hear about it in the coming months.

Alongside its upcoming project slate, CCP-slash-Fenris also revealed a partnership with Google's DeepMind AI division, which the company says "will explore areas including long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning, using Eve Online as a uniquely rich environment for study".

Google has also acquired a minority stake in Fenris as part of this team-up, although it doesn't seem like that stake will affect the company's creative decisions (not yet, at least).

For now, it seems as though Eve Online will continue to operate as it always has, so this change shouldn't impact you too much if you're a longtime fan. Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/eve-online-ccp-games-fenris-creations
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: The Secret of Weepstone Reimagines Classic D&D In This Black And White Dungeon Crawling Adventure - Interview
item.content:encoded:
Dungeons & Dragons-inspired video games have been all the rage as of late, from sweeping epics like Baldur's Gate 3 to the new worlds of Solasta. Having played the demo of The Secret of Weepstone, a new indie D&D-inspired game by Talesworth Game Studio, I was excited to sit down with Sean Gailey to discuss the development of The Secret of Weepstone and his inspirations.

To start at the top, I wanted to ask what the game is, but instead got a bit of a cryptic response on that secret being more than just a title. "Well, that's something you'll have to figure out, I guess," Gailey responded with a smirk on his face.

"As the game starts out, the lord [Oswald] of the town of Weepstone sends the party to figure out what's going on and why his brother is being tormented. They grew up nearby in Weepstone Keep, and they believe that's the source of the problem. They long abandoned the keep when they were kids for reasons you'll learn as the story goes on." 

Oswald asks for six volunteers, "Please go out and figure out what's going on, help my brother," and that's the secret that you have to figure out.

Joking about the many secrets of Weepstone that the players will be able to uncover, I shared my love of games that force me to bring out a corkboard, pad, and pen like Tunic or Animal Well. Gailey had me excited, wishing for me to have "lots of strings and tacks on that corkboard when you're done playing this game."

The Origins Of Weepstone

The Secret of Weepstone is self-professed to be inspired heavily by classic D&D. I asked Gailey about his experience with D&D and what about it drew him to crafting this game.

"I grew up playing D&D in the late '70s/early '80s. Basic/Expert was my first D&D, and what really drew me to that was the artwork. The black-and-white art style of the early books really painted what D&D and fantasy looked like to me. The art of Trampier with his Goblin in the Monster Manual, that is what a Goblin looks like for the rest of my life."

"I always dreamed about making a game that would be like stepping into one of those books. One of the things when you're the Dungeon Master, which I typically was growing up, is that there's so much art in the modules that the player never gets to see. When I was playing, I would be trying to cover things up and show the players the artwork. There were some modules like Tomb of Horrors or the Shrine of Tamoachan where you would get these artbooks, and you could show the players art."

Gailey lamented that these art books weren't more popular, musing that it probably wasn't possible for budget reasons. "That was one of the best things about it, the early, early artists, a lot of them were still new and cutting their teeth. The art isn't perfect, it's not great, they couldn't spend forever on it, so it had to be done quicker and on a budget, and that produced something that felt a little bit like art that you would doodle in your notebook. I loved the imperfections.

"The art also brought challenges to these artists; the art had to be read in small format, they could only be black and white, and that poses a lot of challenges to the artists when they were tasked with creating something and painting a scene that evoked emotion. We have the same challenges when it comes to developing Weepstone. The game is just about all black and white, and that creates a lot of challenges from a game design perspective, from a UI perspective.

"Rather than taking the easy way out when we encounter those challenges, we sit with them until we find something creative. It took a long time to even get it to the prototype stage, but I finally feel like the look will not be changing from now until we launch. A lot of the game systems are still being refined."

Weepstone Isn't a Relationship Simulator, It's a Dungeon Crawl

Having played the demo of Weepstone through a few times, I was really liking how it focused on the dungeon crawl of D&D instead of the grander adventures that some other CRPGs with far larger budgets had recently done.

"D&D now, CRPGs now, they're not better or worse… they're different. I think back to a lot of the great concepts of gaming that went away and came back. I remember when permadeath and hardcore mode re-entered the gaming market. Games were getting easier, easier, easier, and then I don't remember what game it was, but this concept of having a hardcore mode is in every game, but for a while we didn't have that.

"One of the goals for Weepstone was that D&D played a lot differently in the early days, and you'd lose characters, and that was part of the fun. You couldn't reload in tabletop, but the concept of character death is a fun part of role-playing games. It's present for our current tabletop games, but the current iteration of D&D, no one ever really dies, and if you do, you can come back easily."

Talking specifically to the type of CRPG/D&D experience that he's wanting to portray with Weepstone, Gailey explained, "I want to bring this old dungeon crawly, the story is pulpy, there's not a ton of dialogue. It's just a different way to play, and that's the experience I'm hoping to bring."

Death can really be sudden in Weepstone, too. In my first playthrough, I immediately killed one of my party members by having them jump into a dark latrine, which resulted in them falling 70 feet to their death.

What this did reveal to me was the Mortal Favor mechanic. Upon the death of a character, a boon passes on to the rest of the party.

"Whenever anyone dies in Weepstone, and no one really knows why, they convey these boons upon their friends and loved ones and it's different for everybody."

After losing Crig, the homeless man living in a dark corner of the abandoned keep, my party earned the Mastery of None, which gives all characters an additional 3% to succeed on their Ability Tests. Another I got was that for the next three times characters in my party would die, they would instead be left on 1 HP.

Gailey talked about how the Mortal Favors wasn't just a way to help boost the party mechanically, but it's also a way to encourage players to not just think of death as a negative and to be okay losing some party members along the way.

"I know that there are going to be players that do want to reload, I'm always a 'play the way you want,' but if I'm doing my job correctly, then you don't want to reload."

Talking about difficulty and approaches to playing, Gailey did allude to different difficulty modes that will be in the final version of Weepstone, but he did highlight that they didn't want to include modes where you can't ever save or have limited saves. While players might like the challenge, Gailey had the feeling that making saving rarer and reloading more of a chore would impede the overall experience.

"I want it to be fun to lose characters and have the upside be enough that you'll want to keep going."

Losing people will also be balanced with the number of people that you'll be able to add to your party as you continue to explore Weepstone Keep and return to the village. That being said, Gailey also highlighted that the party size can go up to 8, so there's plenty of room to grow and shrink.

Combat in Weepstone Doesn't Need a Text Log

Another aspect of D&D that was streamlined in Weepstone is combat. "I've heard a lot of feedback and comments that they enjoyed having a simplified experience. Everything that you need is shown right there on the screen, and that was a design goal. Let's avoid having a text log and not needing one, keeping it simple."

Gailey also highlighted the difficulty of balancing simple with simplistic. Remarking that, as you get further into the game, they were seriously looking at making sure it didn't get too easy. Looking to add to the original D&D formula, there are also additional items like spell scrolls, which will give players a little bit more magic.

Gailey also wanted to very clearly get across the idea that "Weepstone is not a combat engine. A lot of current CRPGs, the center of the game is combat, and that is not… in fact, that's a bad idea when you're playing. When you have a bunch of low-level nobodies in Basic/Expert, you don't want to be in every combat."

"Combat is straightforward, and a little bit stripped down, but we'll see where it is when it launches."

Knowing how unfavorable the dice can be, I was interested to know what a TPK would look like in Weepstone. Would it eject you from the level and force you to scramble together a new party? Without missing a beat, Gailey joked, "It just uninstalls the game, actually."

Following up with a more serious response, when being faced by a TPK, you'll be forced to restart from a prior save. 

Progression in The Secret of Weepstone

Having picked a party of 'nobodies,' set foot in Weepstone keep, and exploring for an hour or so, I was definitely hooked on the gameplay, but I was interested to hear what the rest of the game has in store for continued exploration as well as character growth.

"Leveling up is in the game, that happens on the character sheet. Following Basic/Expert rules for leveling up, so that is a little bit of a concern for me. In practice, it may feel like not enough is happening at that level up to where it is exciting, that's a bit part of tabletop, the feel of power increases, but it's still in that you'll get more HP, you'll get access to more attacks and spells."

"When you first level up to Level 1, which will probably happen midway through the first level that you play through. Since you start at Level 0, there was a bit of a challenge so I just split up the first level into 1000 exp for each class to get to the first level, and you have latent abilities, latent magic user abilities, and latent Cleric abilities when you're Level 0. When you level up, all of those things activate."

Talking more about time outside the dungeon-crawling gameplay, Gailey told me that there would be plenty of chances to return to the town of Weepstone to rest up, swap out your party, and progress the story. There was emphasis that Weepstone and the surrounding lands weren't massive in scale like Middle-earth, but the Kingdom of Talesworth and some of the lore actually all originates from a book he started to write 20 years ago.

While very different in look, The Secret of Weepstone takes place in the same kingdom as Gailey's previously released Talesworth games: World of Talesworth and Talesworth Adventure: The Lost Artifacts.

"It's a pretty big deal what's going on, but it's not a world thing."

The Secret Of Weepstone Release Window

To wrap up our discussion, I was interested to prod to see if there was a smaller launch window than the 2026 that the Steam page is currently showing. Gailey explained that with the amount of interest and wishlists from Steam, it has really set a fire under those working on the game to make sure it's perfect before sending it out.

"When we were approaching, and then hit 100K wishlists, it's become more and more apparent that this needs to be just done right. We don't want to rush it. You know I'm the sole coder; we do have a very small team. There are lots of great collaborators, great artists, Hunter is writing it, my brother Matt is doing a lot of the level design, and then we have a lot of great artists contributing to it."

"We want to make sure we do it right. Once we get closer, we'll be able to give an official date."

Talking more about the wishlists, Gailey revealed his original hopes for engagement on the Steam platform. "I remember in the beginning, before we even launched the demo or Steam page, my goal was 50k wishlists, so to hit 100k and still have quite a ways until launch, I'm so happy that there is an appetite for a game like this.

"The pressure's on now, I really want to deliver something that is fun for everybody, but I also know when you're playing with nostalgia and memories. The demo is a great example of what this game is. If you like the demo, you're going to like the full game."

The Secret of Weepstone is releasing for PC sometime this year, and there's a demo currently available on Steam that will take you ~45 minutes to get through, where you'll get a bit of the story, a lot of exploration, and some combat. Be sure to go wishlist it too!



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/secret-of-weepstone-reimagines-classic-dd-in-this-black-and-white-dungeon-crawling
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: MindsEye Developer Build a Rocket Boy Hit by Yet More Layoffs
item.content:encoded:
It looks like MindsEye studio Build a Rocket Boy has laid off yet more of its employees, leaving just a relative handful of staff members left to work on the company's ongoing projects.

According to a Kotaku report, which cites "sources familiar with the [latest] restructuring", 170 Build a Rocket Boy employees have lost their job in this round of redundancies, with just around 80 staff members remaining at the studio.

This is, as Kotaku points out, the third time that Build a Rocket Boy has engaged in layoffs in the last twelve months or so. Following the disastrous launch of MindsEye in June last year, the company laid off more than 100 employees, and another round of staff cuts took place in March.

Studio CEO Mark Gerhard blamed the latest round of cuts on "corporate sabotage", a line he's consistently taken in order to explain MindsEye's poor reception. Gerhard appears to believe that MindsEye's spate of negative reviews was caused by a concerted espionage effort rather than, you know, the game not being very good.

In case you missed all of this, MindsEye was released in June last year, and its launch was messy, to say the least, with influencer partnerships being dropped on day one and irate customers demanding refunds from platforms (which were granted in many cases).

The game's launch was so bad, in fact, that it reportedly prompted publisher IO Interactive to exit the third-party publishing game altogether. Build a Rocket Boy split with IO Interactive earlier this year, choosing instead to self-publish MindsEye going forward.

Build a Rocket Boy's company culture doesn't appear to be particularly savory, either; a group of employees recently filed a lawsuit against the studio, alleging unauthorized surveillance.

If you want to check out MindsEye to see what all the fuss is about, it's available now on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S, although our very own Sam would caution against doing so for all but the stoutest of heart.
